explanation: The property describes that anytime a MID message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must forward this message to the Duplicate Set. Then, if the message is not already registered (not expressible in LTL with the provided atomic propositions), the Duplicate Set registers the message. Since LTL cannot check stateful information like 'not registered' without a dedicated proposition, and only the registration action is available as an atomic proposition, we simply require that after forwarding the message to the Duplicate Set, either registration by the Duplicate Set happens, or not (empty alternative), which in LTL equates to it being optional after the forwarding. Thus, the main enforceable causality is: if a MID is sent and is forwarded to Duplicate Set, then optionally a registration can follow. This is encoded as: 'G (mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set & (F registration_performed_by_duplicate_set | true)))'. Since '| true' makes it vacuously true, it's unnecessary, so the best enforceable property is: globally, when a MID message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, it is eventually forwarded to the Duplicate Set, and if not yet registered is handled inside the Duplicate Set component (which may not be directly expressible here). Thus: G (mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set) is the best fit.

The property described requires that whenever a MID message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, it must (eventually) be forwarded to the Duplicate Set, and, if the message is not yet registered, there should be a registration operation performed by the Duplicate Set. The alternative operator with an empty alternative means that the registration by the Duplicate Set occurs only if the message is not registered, but there is no explicit negation in the atomic propositions set, so the closest translation without such propositions uses implication: after forwarding, if registration is needed (implicitly modeled as a possibility), it will eventually happen (registration is possible but not mandatory unless 'not registered'). The LTL formula expresses that globally, whenever a MID message is sent to Local Node, eventually it is forwarded to the Duplicate Set, and once forwarded, either no registration is needed (nothing more happens) or registration is performed after forwarding. We combine these using implication and 'until' to chain the expected behaviors.

The input natural language describes that whenever a MID message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must forward the message to the Duplicate Set component. Then, if the message was not yet registered, the Duplicate Set component performs a registration. No registration will occur if the message is already registered, aligning with the 'alternative with empty alternative' description—i.e., registration may or may not occur, depending on registration status. The property is expected to hold for every DM_i (general case).

The most accurate LTL formulation from the available atomic propositions is:
Globally, whenever a MID message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node (mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), it is eventually forwarded to the Duplicate Set component (msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set), and then (optionally) a registration is performed by the Duplicate Set component (registration_performed_by_duplicate_set), if the message is not registered. To encode the optional registration, simply say that registration_performed_by_duplicate_set can happen any time after the forwarding, but it's not required to always happen.

Thus, for every occurrence of mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node, eventually msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set, and after that, optionally registration_performed_by_duplicate_set (which may or may not occur).

This can be encoded as:
G (mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set & ( (registration_performed_by_duplicate_set) | G !registration_performed_by_duplicate_set) ) )

However, since we do not know the registered/not registered state, the best possible formalization (with current atomic propositions) is that, after every mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node and forwarding to duplicate_set, registration either happens or does not. This formula allows both cases due to absence of more fine-grained state.

Other LTL expressions may miss the optional nature of registration (always requiring it, which would be incorrect), or not connect the forwarding with registration.

The natural language states that for every MID message delivered from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must forward the message to the Duplicate Set; if the message is not registered yet, the Duplicate Set registers it (otherwise, nothing special happens: 'empty alternative'). This 'alternative operator with empty alternative' essentially encodes as 'if the message wasn't registered, there must be a registration; otherwise, things continue.' In LTL, we express that whenever a 'mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' occurs, it must eventually be followed by 'msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set', and then (optionally) by 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' (if not already registered, which is abstracted away here because we cannot test registration directly - so we allow both possibilities). Modeling this in plain LTL with only atomic props, a strict encoding is: whenever a 'mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' occurs, 'msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set' must eventually follow, and (optionally) be followed by 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' if needed. So we can say globally: whenever 'mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' happens, eventually 'msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set' happens, and after that, 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' may or may not happen (but if it is needed, it must). Given that we cannot encode history ("if not registered") with only the given atomic props, we can only require that forwarding to Duplicate Set is always followed by optional registration. Thus, the most faithful possible expression is: G(mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set & (F(registration_performed_by_duplicate_set) | true))). This can be slightly simplified by omitting the always-true 'or': G(mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set)). That is, every time a MID message is sent to the Local Node, it is eventually forwarded to the Duplicate Set. Optionally, we can add that after 'msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set', possibly 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' occurs, but since we can't check the condition (message not yet registered), we must leave it at that.

The property describes that whenever a MID message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node eventually forwards it to the Duplicate Set, and if the message is not registered (which we represent by the registration_performed_by_duplicate_set atomic proposition being true), then registration happens. Since only atomic propositions are allowed, we assume 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' covers the registration upon first arrival. The property should express that for every occurrence of 'mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node', eventually both the forwarding and (optionally) the registration occur—the latter only if not yet registered. The closest matching formalization with atomic propositions is that after MID is sent, eventually the forward and (if needed) registration both happen; but since we can't express 'if not yet registered', we'll require that after a MID is sent, forwarding and registration eventually happen (allowing that registration may be vacuously true if unnecessary). Therefore, the most representative property is: `G(mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set & (registration_performed_by_duplicate_set | X(true))))`. Here, `X(true)` simply covers the 'empty alternative' to allow 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' to be skipped if the message is already registered, within the constraints of pure LTL on atomic propositions. This matches typical LTL patterns for 'send then process' message flows as described.

The natural language states that whenever the Local Node receives a MID message from DM_i, it must forward that message to the Duplicate Set component. Subsequently, if the message is not yet registered, the Duplicate Set component should register the message. The property should hold for every DM_i. The translation requires expressing that, globally, whenever a 'mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' occurs, it is eventually followed by 'msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set', and then, optionally (since registration only occurs if not yet registered), either 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' or nothing (the alternative operator with an empty alternative). In LTL, this is captured by nesting the eventual operator for 'msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set' following 'mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node', and then after that, allowing for the optional registration to occur. The release operator (V) is used to model the alternative with the empty alternative: after forwarding, either registration occurs, or nothing needs to happen until the next instance. Thus, the property is captured as: G(mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set & (F registration_performed_by_duplicate_set | true))). This reads as: globally, if a MID message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, then eventually (at some later point) the message is forwarded to the Duplicate Set, and either eventually a registration occurs, or nothing else must occur (the empty alternative).

The property describes the following: When a MID message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, it must be forwarded to the Duplicate Set component, and if the message is not yet registered, the Duplicate Set registers it. The property is meant to apply to every MID message. In LTL, since there is no explicit atomic proposition specifying 'the message is not yet registered,' we can only express that if a MID message is sent to the Local Node, then eventually it is forwarded to the Duplicate Set and then (optionally, if applicable) registration is performed. The 'alternative operator with an empty alternative' corresponds to standard LTL implication: the registration may not happen if the message is already present, so registration is not strictly required every time, but forwarding always is. The best we can encode without extra atomic propositions is: for every occurrence of 'mid_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node,' eventually 'msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set' occurs, and optionally, 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' may follow. Also, to maintain the order, we use the Until operator: after forwarding, we do not require registration, but if it occurs, it does so after forwarding. Hence, the formula expresses: whenever a MID message is sent, it is eventually forwarded to the Duplicate Set, and possibly (if relevant) registration happens. This captures the intent described. Previous approaches that strictly require registration every time would be too strong and not account for the case of an already-registered message; approaches that do not require forwarding at all would be too weak.

The natural language states that whenever the Local Node forwards a MID message to the Duplicate Set component, then, if the message is not yet registered, the Duplicate Set registers it. Modeling this with 'alternative operator with an empty alternative' suggests using the 'until' (U) operator: the property holds until something happens, and if it never happens, it is always true. In LTL, we capture that every time a message is forwarded to the Duplicate Set, eventually either it is registered or nothing happens (if it's already registered). But since we can't use numeric comparisons or 'already registered' as a proposition, we must only guarantee that whenever 'msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set' occurs, then either eventually 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' occurs, or not. To be precise, the property is focusing on the obligation: if the message is not already registered, then registration occurs. Since we lack a proposition for 'not yet registered', we encode that whenever a message is forwarded to the Duplicate Set, it is eventually registered by Duplicate Set. Thus, the most likely translation is: globally, whenever 'msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set' happens, eventually 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' follows. This captures the intent that if registration needs to happen, it must happen after forwarding. There is no previous LTL property to criticize.

The property described states that whenever the Local Node component forwards a MID message to the Duplicate Set component, the Duplicate Set should (if the message is not yet registered) register the message. In the absence of an explicit atomic proposition for 'the message is not yet registered,' the LTL property should focus on the obligation that 'whenever a MID message is forwarded to Duplicate Set, it is either already registered or gets registered.' We only have the proposition 'registration_performed_by_duplicate_set' for the registration action, so we express that whenever a MID message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node and then forwarded to the Duplicate Set, eventually the Duplicate Set will perform registration. To capture the 'alternative operator with empty alternative' logic, we can use 'msg_forwarded_to_duplicate_set -> F registration_performed_by_duplicate_set', under the assumption that registration only happens if the message was not already registered (as deduced from the atomic proposition descriptions). The property says that globally, whenever a MID message is forwarded to the Duplicate Set, registration occurs eventually (i.e., if needed).

Previous properties (if using implication only on the initial msg sent, or not relating the forwarding to the registration) may fail to connect the forwarding and the registration action or accidentally allow misordered behaviors. This LTL captures exactly the stated obligation.

The natural language describes that whenever a MID message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, then the Local Node must forward the message to the Duplicate Set component, and then, if the message is not yet registered, the Duplicate Set performs registration. We can best represent the strict part (the forwarding) by a response property: globally, whenever a MID message is sent, eventually it is forwarded to the Duplicate Set component. The registration by the Duplicate Set, conditional on non-registration, is not directly representable in LTL with only the provided atomic propositions, since we do not have an atomic proposition for 'message is not yet registered'. So, we encode the requirement that a send must lead to a forward, and if further, a registration may occur, but only if modelled in the underlying system.

Why is this LTL formula apt? It captures the causality of MID message arrival and the necessity of forwarding to the Duplicate Set, which is the main enforceable part given the atomic propositions. Previous encodings that might have only required that the forwarded-to-Duplicate-Set action happens at least once would have missed the input's per-message causality. Prior encodings requiring 'registration' after the forward would add assumptions we cannot formalize (about message registration state) without further atomic propositions. This formula enforces the reaction per occurrence of the MID message, as specified.
